aliases.name: Ibukidōji
aliases.meaning: Mount Ibuki version of the legend

description.summary: Shuten-dōji ('sake-drinking lad') is a legendary oni (demon) of Japanese folklore who, set in the reign of Emperor Ichijō (986–1011), is said to have led a band of demons from a fortress on Mount Ōe and abducted nobles of the capital. He was defeated by the warrior Minamoto no Yorimitsu (Raikō) and his Four Heavenly Kings, who infiltrated his stronghold disguised as yamabushi and gave him a poisoned sake.
description.full: Shuten-dōji (酒呑童子, 'sake-drinking lad') is one of the most famous oni of Japanese legend. The story is set in the reign of Emperor Ichijō (986–1011) and casts the demon as the king of the oni of Mount Ōe (variously identified as Ōeyama in northwestern Kyoto / on the Tango–Tamba border, or with the southern Ōe near Kameoka). From this stronghold the demon and his followers are said to have raided the capital and carried off members of the nobility. By imperial command, the warrior Minamoto no Yorimitsu — popularly known as Raikō — together with his Four Heavenly Kings (shitennō: Watanabe no Tsuna, Sakata no Kintoki, Urabe no Suetake, and Usui Sadamitsu) and the courtier Fujiwara no Yasumasa, set out to destroy him. Disguising themselves as yamabushi (mountain ascetics) and aided by deities of Sumiyoshi, Iwashimizu Hachimangū, and Kumano (depending on the version), the heroes were welcomed into the demon's hall, where they presented him with a 'divine elixir' — a poisoned sake — that incapacitated him. Once he had drunk himself into a stupor they cut off his head, which is said to have flown through the air and tried to bite Raikō before being held down. The earliest surviving illustrated text, the Ōeyama Ekotoba (Ōeyama Picture Scroll), is held by the Itsuō Art Museum and dates to the 14th century. The legend was carried into Otogi-zōshi short stories, the Otogi Bunko of the early 18th century, Noh and Kabuki theatre, ukiyo-e, and into modern manga, anime, and video games. Major variant texts include Ōeyama Shuten-dōji and Ibukiyama Shuten-dōji, which place the events at different mountains and offer different origin stories for the demon.

etymology.origin: Japanese: 酒 shu ('sake'), 呑 ten ('to drink'), 童子 dōji ('boy / child / acolyte').
etymology.meaning: 'Sake-drinking lad', referring to the demon's love of sake.
etymology.first_use: 14th century (in the Ōeyama Ekotoba); the legend is set earlier, in the Heian period (Emperor Ichijō, r. 986–1011).

related_phenomena.name: Ibaraki-dōji
related_phenomena.description: Shuten-dōji's chief retainer; severed-arm legend involving Watanabe no Tsuna at Rashōmon

related_phenomena.name: Tsuchigumo
related_phenomena.description: Another monstrous adversary of Minamoto no Yorimitsu

related_phenomena.name: Oni
related_phenomena.description: General class of Japanese demons; Shuten-dōji is their archetypal king

related_phenomena.name: Yamamba
related_phenomena.description: Mountain hag identified in some versions as Shuten-dōji's mother

features.description: Pictured with one or more horns and dishevelled hair, in the standard oni iconography.
Depicted as gigantic; in the most exaggerated versions, several meters tall with a head able to fly.
Despite his demonic nature, he is conventionally depicted with a youthful or boyish face — the dōji ('lad') of his name.
Standard oni colouration in pictorial tradition.

physical.morphology: Oni morphology: humanoid, horned, often clawed and fanged, capable of shape-shifting into a beautiful young man to deceive humans.

abilities.description: Possesses prodigious physical strength, said to be invulnerable to ordinary weapons.
Rules a band of subordinate oni from his Mount Ōe fortress.
Can disguise himself as a beautiful young nobleman or ascetic; his hall appears as a sumptuous palace.
After decapitation, his head flies through the air to bite Raikō, requiring multiple warriors to hold it down.

behavior.diet: Meat and blood of human victims; insatiable for sake
patterns: Raids the capital from a mountain fortress
Holds demonic banquets and consumes vast quantities of sake
Rules subordinate oni with strategic intelligence

range.description: Mountain regions north and northwest of the Heian capital (Kyoto): Mount Ōe (Ōeyama) on the border of Tango and Tamba provinces; Mount Ibuki on the Ōmi–Mino border; and the southern Ōe area near Kameoka in modern Kyoto Prefecture.

hotspots.name: Mount Ōe (Ōeyama)

location.description: Mountain on the Tango–Tamba border in northern Kyoto Prefecture; principal setting of the Shuten-dōji legend

hotspots.notes: Site of the Ōeyama Ekotoba scroll's narrative; today hosts a Shuten-dōji-themed museum.

hotspots.name: Mount Ibuki

location.description: Mountain on the Ōmi–Mino border (modern Shiga / Gifu Prefectures); setting of the Ibukiyama Shuten-dōji variant

hotspots.notes: Variant tradition giving Shuten-dōji a different origin story (sometimes as a child of the deity of Mount Ibuki).

peak_activity_periods.description: Legend reproduced in Otogi-zōshi short stories, Otogi Bunko (c. 1720), Noh, Kabuki, and ukiyo-e.

origins.cultural_roots: Heian-court legend literature, mountain-ascetic (yamabushi) folklore, and a long oral tradition of oni stories of the central Japanese highlands.
origins.earliest_accounts: The Ōeyama Ekotoba illustrated scroll (14th century), held by the Itsuō Art Museum, is the earliest surviving textual form.
origins.folklore_connections: Connected to other Yorimitsu cycle legends (Tsuchigumo, Ibaraki-dōji at Rashōmon) and to a wider Japanese tradition of mountain-dwelling oni and demon kings.

timeline.event: Beginning of the reign of Emperor Ichijō, the legendary setting of the Shuten-dōji story
timeline.significance: Anchors the legend in the high Heian court

timeline.event: Ōeyama Ekotoba illustrated scroll produced (now held by the Itsuō Art Museum)
timeline.significance: Earliest surviving narrative form of the legend

timeline.event: Shuten-dōji story circulates as one of the canonical Otogi-zōshi short stories
Shuten-dōji included in the Otogi Bunko woodblock-printed collection of 23 stories
timeline.significance: Mass popular distribution in early modern Japan

timeline.event: Shuten-dōji depicted in ukiyo-e prints (e.g. by Utagawa Kuniyoshi) and adapted for Noh, Kabuki, and bunraku stages
timeline.significance: Establishes the iconic Edo visual tradition

history.modern_era: The Shuten-dōji legend remains a cornerstone of Japanese popular culture, recurring in manga, anime, video games (notably the Onmyōji and Fate franchises), and themed local tourism around Mount Ōe.

documentary.name: Ōeyama Ekotoba
documentary.description: Earliest surviving illustrated text of the Shuten-dōji legend, held by the Itsuō Art Museum (Ikeda, Osaka).
documentary.analysis: Foundational source for all later versions; the Itsuō scroll is the principal scholarly reference.

documentary.name: Otogi Bunko (companion library)
documentary.description: Edo-period woodblock-printed collection of 23 otogi-zōshi tales, including Shuten-dōji.
documentary.analysis: Major distribution vector for the legend in early modern Japan.

paranormal.name: Real demon king of Heian Japan
paranormal.description: Within tradition, Shuten-dōji is treated as a real demon-king who menaced the Heian capital and was destroyed by Yorimitsu's expedition.
evidence_cited: Continuity of legend across Otogi-zōshi, Noh, and Kabuki

skeptical.name: Allegory for marginalised peoples and bandits
skeptical.description: Modern scholarship has read Shuten-dōji as a folkloric figure encoding Heian and medieval anxieties about non-court populations of the mountains — bandits, mountain ascetics, ironworking communities, and groups outside the capital's authority — recast as 'demons'.
evidence_cited: Late medieval origin texts coinciding with court anxieties about provincial unrest
Variant traditions placing Shuten-dōji's origin in mountain-deity or marginalised-community ancestry

skeptical.name: Heroic legitimation of the Minamoto
skeptical.description: The Yorimitsu cycle, including Shuten-dōji, functioned to legitimise the warrior aristocracy of the Minamoto and their service to the imperial court.
evidence_cited: Recurrent pairing of Yorimitsu's exploits in late medieval literature
Otogi-zōshi and Noh treatments emphasising imperial mandate

traditions: Yorimitsu (Raikō) and Four Heavenly Kings cycle
folklore.beliefs: Treated in tradition as a paradigm of demonic evil and as a foundational tale of warrior heroism and imperial legitimacy.

literature: Reider, Noriko T., Japanese Demon Lore: Oni from Ancient Times to the Present (Utah State University Press, 2010)
Reider, Noriko T., Seven Demon Stories from Medieval Japan (Utah State University Press, 2016)
film_tv: Numerous anime adaptations (e.g. depictions in 'Otogi Zoshi', 'Onmyōji' series, and several jidaigeki films)
games: Onmyōji (mobile game by NetEase) — Shuten-dōji as a major shikigami
Fate/Grand Order (Type-Moon) — Shuten-dōji appears as a Servant
other: Mount Ōe Shuten-dōji-themed local museum and festival in Fukuchiyama, Kyoto Prefecture
Edo-period ukiyo-e prints by Utagawa Kuniyoshi

researchers.name: Noriko T. Reider
researchers.contribution: Major Anglophone scholarship on oni and the Shuten-dōji corpus

researchers.name: Komatsu Kazuhiko
researchers.affiliation: International Research Center for Japanese Studies (Nichibunken)
researchers.contribution: Foundational Japanese scholarship on oni and yōkai

organizations.name: International Research Center for Japanese Studies (Nichibunken)
organizations.focus: Yōkai and folklore database, including Shuten-dōji material

organizations.name: Itsuō Art Museum
organizations.focus: Holder of the Ōeyama Ekotoba scroll

research.ongoing_studies: Active scholarship on the Shuten-dōji corpus includes critical editions of the medieval scrolls, comparative oni studies, and cultural-history work on warrior-aristocracy legitimation.

sources.title: Japanese Demon Lore: Oni from Ancient Times to the Present
Seven Demon Stories from Medieval Japan
